ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for katsudon; mapped to the closest parent class 'rice bowl dish' (FOODON_03311069). A more specific katsudon term is absent from the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pork dish — composite cooked dish with rice, pork, and egg; mapped to FoodEx2 composite dish category for pork-based rice bowls. Exact katsudon code not present; A0FJP (pork, cooked) used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a dedicated katsudon entry. A0FJP (cooked pork) used as the primary ingredient anchor. Composite dish coding with rice (A000N) and egg (A00LC) components acknowledged.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for katsudon or Japanese rice bowl dishes as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of katsudon weighs approximately 550 g, comprising steamed rice, a breaded deep-fried pork cutlet, simmered egg-and-onion topping, and dashi-based sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pork, fresh, loin, whole, separable lean and fat, cooked, braised (FDC ID 168319); White Rice, cooked (FDC ID 168878); Egg, whole, cooked, fried (FDC ID 173424); composite estimation for katsudon
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for katsudon as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked white rice (~55% by weight), deep-fried breaded pork cutlet (~25%), simmered egg (~12%), onion (~5%), and dashi-soy sauce (~3%). The input record's per-100g values (energy 154.55 kcal, protein 8.18 g, fat 6.36 g, carbohydrate 15.45 g) are consistent with this composite estimation and have been retained. Sodium at 218 mg/100g reflects soy sauce and dashi contribution and is plausible. All values cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central component entries.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — White rice, boiled (GI ~72)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for katsudon as a composite dish. GI of 72 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate component: Japanese short-grain white rice, boiled, which has a published GI of approximately 72 (high) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The pork cutlet, egg, and sauce components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (72 × 85.0) / 100 ≈ 61.3, where carbohydrate per 550 g serving = 15.45 × 5.5 = 84.975 g ≈ 85.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from soy sauce, dashi broth, and mirin; a full 550 g serving delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit katsudon to occasional consumption, request reduced-sodium soy sauce and minimal dashi broth, and avoid consuming the full sauce to keep sodium intake below 600 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI ~72) with 15.45 g carbohydrate per 100 g and low dietary fibre (0.55 g/100 g) produces a high glycaemic load of ~61 per standard serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit katsudon due to its high glycaemic load; substituting brown rice, reducing portion size to 200–250 g, and requesting preparation without added mirin or sugar can partially mitigate postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying generates trans fats (0.09 g/100 g) and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); saturated fat at 1.82 g/100 g and cholesterol at 45.45 mg/100 g contribute to atherogenic risk; high sodium further elevates cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular katsudon consumption; if consumed, portion should be limited to 200 g or less, and preparation should be modified to baked or grilled pork cutlet with reduced-sodium sauce to lower saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying produces pro-inflammatory AGEs; pork contributes arachidonic acid; no meaningful anti-inflammatory components present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried katsudon; a modified version using baked or grilled pork or tofu with omega-3-rich additions would be preferable to reduce AGE burden and arachidonic acid intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Katsudon (カツ丼) is a classic Japanese donburi (rice bowl) dish consisting of a deep-fried breaded pork cutlet (tonkatsu) simmered together with beaten egg and sliced onion in a savoury-sweet dashi-based sauce made with soy sauce, mirin, and sugar, served over a bed of steamed Japanese short-grain white rice. The dish is a staple of Japanese home cooking and casual restaurant dining, widely popular across Japan and in Japanese restaurants throughout Southeast Asia and Singapore. The pork cutlet is first coated in flour, egg wash, and panko breadcrumbs before being deep-fried until golden and crispy; it is then briefly simmered in the sauce with onion and egg until the egg is just set, creating a soft, custardy topping that contrasts with the crispy pork. The flavour profile is umami-rich, mildly sweet, and savoury, with the dashi providing depth and the mirin contributing a subtle sweetness. Nutritionally, katsudon is a calorie-dense dish at approximately 155 kcal per 100 g, providing 8.2 g protein, 6.4 g fat, and 15.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g. A standard 550 g bowl delivers approximately 850 kcal, 45 g protein, 35 g fat, and 85 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (~218 mg/100 g; ~1200 mg per serving) due to soy sauce and dashi, and has a high glycaemic index (~72) driven by the white rice base, making it unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, or cardiovascular disease without significant modification. The deep-frying process also generates trans fats and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which are of concern for inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis.
common_user_queries: What is in a katsudon bowl?
Can I eat katsudon with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for the component ingredients (white rice, deep-fried breaded pork, egg, onion, soy-dashi sauce). No single approved-source entry for katsudon as a composite dish exists. The input values from the GrabFood-sourced record are consistent with the USDA composite estimation and have been retained. GI is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate component (white rice, GI ~72) per the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact katsudon entry exists in any approved primary source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for katsudon as a composite dish are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source as of 2024. Values could be estimated from component-level USDA entries but have not been computed here to avoid compounding estimation error. trans_fat_g of 0.09 g/100g is retained from the input record and is plausible for a deep-fried preparation. All core macros (protein_g 8.18, fat_g 6.36, carbohydrate_g 15.45) are non-zero and consistent with a composite rice-and-pork dish; no zero-macro justification is required. Country of origin in the input record was listed as Singapore, which has been corrected to Japan as katsudon is a Japanese dish; the dish is served in Singapore but originates in Japan. The ethnic_tags entry 'Sashimi' in the input has been removed as it is incorrect for katsudon. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source per University of Sydney GI Database.
